context: TypeScript + React Next.js app. .claude/bruhs.json has framework='nextjs'. src/components/ contains 45 files, including a UserCard.tsx with a derived-state useEffect anti-pattern, a Form.tsx with an `any` type on API response, and a HeroImage.tsx with an unstable inline style object causing re-renders.
expected_behavior: Step 1 reads .claude/bruhs.json to learn framework=nextjs, Step 4 loads practices/type-driven-design.md (PRIMARY), practices/_common.md, and practices/typescript-react.md
Analyzes src/components following the priority hierarchy: Types (P1) > Security (P2) > Performance anti-patterns (P3) > Error Handling (P4) > Architecture (P5) > Immutability (P6) > Style (P7)

Flags the `any` on the API response as a P1 type-signature violation (highest priority), the unstable inline style reference as a P3 performance anti-pattern (not a style nit), and the derived-state useEffect as a React anti-pattern
Generates the report grouped by severity (Critical / High / Medium / Low) with a summary table counting issues per category
Follows up with AskUserQuestion asking whether to fix issues interactively or generate a report only
Does NOT auto-fix behavior-changing issues — only unused imports, console.logs, and redundant type annotations are safe auto-fix candidates per the command doc
failure_modes: Flags the performance anti-patterns (unstable references, N+1) as low-priority style nits instead of high-priority bugs, violating the priority hierarchy
Runs auto-fix on type changes without prompting, silently altering behavior
Skips loading practices/typescript-react.md despite framework=nextjs, missing React-specific anti-patterns
